heroOverview: Panuveitis is inflammation of all three anatomical layers of the uveal tract — the iris, ciliary body, and choroid — simultaneously, involving both the anterior and posterior segments of the eye. When both eyes are affected, the designation is bilateral panuveitis (ICD-10: H44.113). Panuveitis accounts for roughly 7-10% of all uveitis presentations but carries a disproportionate burden of vision loss: approximately 35% of patients experience significant visual impairment in at least one eye over a five-year period. It is nearly always driven by a systemic inflammatory or infectious cause — sarcoidosis, Vogt-Koyanagi-Harada disease, Behçet disease, tuberculosis, and syphilis are among the most important — and the bilateral pattern, in particular, signals a high probability of an identifiable systemic aetiology. Identifying and treating the underlying condition is as important as controlling ocular inflammation to preserve sight.

keyStats.prevalence: Panuveitis represents ~7-10% of all uveitis cases; uveitis overall affects approximately 38-115 per 100,000 persons (Foster CS et al., 2010)
keyStats.demographics: Peak incidence in adults aged 20-60; bilateral forms strongly associated with systemic diseases more prevalent in this age range
keyStats.avgAge: Most common in adults aged 20-50 years; Vogt-Koyanagi-Harada disease peaks in the third to fifth decades
keyStats.globalCases: Uveitis causes approximately 5-10% of legal blindness in developed countries (AAO, 2021)

definition: Panuveitis (ICD-10: H44.113 — bilateral) is anatomically defined as concurrent active inflammation of the anterior uvea (iris and ciliary body, producing iridocyclitis), the vitreous, and the posterior uvea (choroid and adjacent retinal pigment epithelium, producing choroiditis or chorioretinitis). When both eyes are involved simultaneously, the condition is bilateral panuveitis. Classification systems developed by the Standardization of Uveitis Nomenclature (SUN) working group grade inflammation by cell count and flare in the anterior chamber, vitreous haze, and posterior segment lesions. The differential diagnosis is broad: infectious causes include tuberculosis, syphilis, toxoplasmosis, cytomegalovirus, and herpesviruses; non-infectious immune-mediated causes include sarcoidosis, Behçet disease, Vogt-Koyanagi-Harada (VKH) disease, systemic lupus erythematosus, and sympathetic ophthalmia following ocular trauma. The bilateral pattern substantially narrows the differential toward VKH, sarcoidosis, Behçet disease, syphilis, and tuberculosis. Prompt identification of the aetiology is essential because treatment differs markedly between infectious and immune-mediated causes. The condition falls within the specialty of ophthalmology, with frequent co-management by rheumatology, infectious disease, or immunology depending on the systemic diagnosis.

typesClassification.description: Caused by organisms reaching the uveal tract haematogenously or directly, including M. tuberculosis, Treponema pallidum (syphilis), Toxoplasma gondii, herpesviruses (HSV, VZV, CMV), and Borrelia burgdorferi. Treatment is pathogen-specific; corticosteroids are used adjunctively with caution.

An autoimmune T-cell-mediated attack on melanocyte-containing tissues producing bilateral granulomatous panuveitis, auditory and neurological symptoms, and depigmentation of skin and hair. Among the most common causes of bilateral panuveitis in Asian, Hispanic, Native American, and Middle Eastern populations.

Granulomatous inflammation from sarcoidosis affecting all uveal segments; typically bilateral, insidious, and associated with mutton-fat keratic precipitates, Dalen-Fuchs nodules in the retina, and systemic features such as hilar lymphadenopathy and elevated ACE.

Severe, recurrent occlusive vasculitis-driven uveitis with hypopyon formation and retinal vessel occlusion; primarily affects young adults of Middle Eastern, Central Asian, and East Asian descent and is one of the most visually destructive forms of uveitis.

A rare but devastating bilateral granulomatous panuveitis triggered by penetrating trauma or surgery to one eye, representing an autoimmune response to uveal antigens exposed to the immune system. The uninvolved eye is the one at greatest risk of blindness.

primarySymptoms: Blurred vision in one or both eyes, often the presenting complaint, resulting from anterior chamber cells, vitreous haze, or macular oedema.
Ocular pain and aching around the eye, most pronounced with anterior segment involvement affecting the ciliary muscle.
Photophobia — painful sensitivity to light — caused by ciliary spasm and iris inflammation.
Floaters in the visual field, produced by inflammatory cells and debris in the vitreous gel.
Redness of the eye (ciliary injection), particularly around the limbus (the corneal-scleral junction).
Decreased contrast sensitivity and colour perception from macular or posterior pole involvement.
Lacrimation and tearing from ocular surface irritation and reflex response to light sensitivity.
Central or paracentral scotoma (blind spot) when the macula or optic nerve is involved by choroiditis or macular oedema.
Headache and scalp tenderness, characteristic of Vogt-Koyanagi-Harada disease's associated meningeal inflammation.
Hearing changes (tinnitus, mild hearing loss) or skin depigmentation (vitiligo, poliosis) as systemic clues to VKH disease.
earlyWarningSigns: New floaters appearing in one or both eyes, especially alongside any visual blurring, even if mild.
Light sensitivity developing alongside a red, achy eye — early ciliary involvement before vision is clearly reduced.
Gradual haziness of vision not corrected by blinking, suggesting vitreous or posterior segment involvement.
Unexplained new-onset headache with tinnitus or ringing in the ears in a young adult — a possible early signal of VKH disease.
emergencySigns: Sudden severe loss of vision in one or both eyes, which may indicate retinal detachment, dense vitreous haemorrhage, or massive choroidal effusion.
Eye pain with a markedly hard eyeball and very blurred vision — a presentation of acute angle-closure glaucoma complicating panuveitis requiring emergency treatment.
Hypopyon visible as a white fluid level in the lower anterior chamber — a sign of intense inflammation, particularly in Behçet disease.
Bilateral visual loss accompanied by headache and meningism — suggests VKH disease with meningeal involvement requiring urgent systemic workup.

causes.description: Non-caseating granulomatous inflammation from sarcoidosis involves the uvea in 25-50% of patients with ocular sarcoidosis. Activated macrophages and T-helper lymphocytes form granulomata at all uveal levels, producing bilateral panuveitis with a chronic or recurrent course. Elevated serum angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) and lysozyme, and chest CT findings, support the diagnosis.

An autoimmune attack on melanocyte-bearing tissues mediated by CD4+ T lymphocytes targeting tyrosinase-related protein antigens. The inflammation involves the uveal tract of both eyes simultaneously, the meninges, inner ear, and skin. VKH is the most common cause of bilateral panuveitis in Asian, Latin American, and Middle Eastern populations.

A systemic vasculitis of uncertain aetiology characterised by recurrent oral aphthous ulcers, genital ulcers, and ocular inflammation. The ocular inflammation is an obliterative retinal vasculitis driving ischaemia; anterior hypopyon forms from intense anterior chamber exudation. Each attack causes cumulative vascular occlusion and progressive vision loss.

Ocular TB may result from direct haematogenous seeding of the choroid or from a delayed hypersensitivity reaction to mycobacterial antigens at a remote pulmonary focus. It produces granulomatous bilateral posterior and panuveitis with serpiginous choroiditis patterns and is an important diagnosis in endemic regions.

Secondary or tertiary syphilis can produce any pattern of uveitis including bilateral panuveitis. Treponema pallidum invades uveal tissue directly; the diagnosis is made by serology (VDRL/RPR and FTA-ABS). Syphilitic uveitis is fully reversible with adequate penicillin therapy and must be excluded in every uveitis workup.

Penetrating trauma or surgery exposing uveal antigens to the immune system triggers a bilateral granulomatous panuveitis in which the healthy eye (the sympathising eye) is attacked by sensitised T lymphocytes. It can develop weeks to decades after the sensitising injury.

riskFactors.description: Diagnosed sarcoidosis, Behçet disease, systemic lupus erythematosus, ankylosing spondylitis, and inflammatory bowel disease substantially elevate the risk of developing panuveitis compared with the general population.

VKH disease is 3-4 times more common in individuals of Asian, Middle Eastern, Hispanic, and Native American heritage, associated with HLA-DR4 and HLA-DRB1 alleles. Behçet disease is more prevalent along the historical Silk Road countries.

HIV infection and other immunodeficiencies allow opportunistic organisms — CMV, Toxoplasma, fungi, syphilis — to produce bilateral panuveitis. Paradoxically, immune reconstitution in HIV patients starting antiretrovirals can trigger acute uveitis.

Any penetrating ocular injury or intraocular surgery can sensitise the immune system to uveal antigens, triggering sympathetic ophthalmia in the fellow eye weeks to years later. Enucleation of the injured eye within two weeks may prevent sympathetic ophthalmia from developing.
Living in or travelling to high-burden TB regions, or having untreated syphilis (particularly in HIV co-infected individuals), raises the risk of infectious bilateral panuveitis.
HLA-B51 is strongly associated with Behçet disease, conferring a relative risk approximately 6 times the background rate; the association is strongest in Middle Eastern and Asian populations.

Each episode of untreated or inadequately treated panuveitis causes cumulative damage to the retina, optic nerve, and lens, reducing the probability of full visual recovery. Early aggressive treatment is the most important modifiable factor for long-term visual prognosis.

affectedDemographics: Adults aged 20-50 years, the peak age range for most systemic inflammatory causes
Women slightly more often than men for VKH disease; men slightly more often than women for Behçet disease
Individuals of Asian, Middle Eastern, Hispanic, and Native American descent for VKH and Behçet disease
HIV-positive individuals, particularly those with low CD4 counts, due to infectious causes
People with established systemic inflammatory diseases such as sarcoidosis or SLE
Individuals who have had penetrating ocular trauma (sympathetic ophthalmia risk)
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosing bilateral panuveitis requires a slit-lamp examination of both anterior and posterior segments, combined with a targeted systemic workup to identify an underlying aetiology. At the slit lamp, the ophthalmologist quantifies anterior chamber cells and flare, examines keratic precipitates (mutton-fat precipitates suggest granulomatous causes such as sarcoidosis or TB), assesses vitreous haze grading, and examines the fundus for choroidal lesions, retinal vasculitis, disc oedema, or exudative retinal detachment. Optical coherence tomography (OCT) is essential for detecting and quantifying macular oedema, the most common cause of central visual loss in panuveitis. Fluorescein angiography documents retinal vascular leakage and ischaemia; indocyanine green angiography reveals choroidal stromal lesions not visible on fluorescein. A comprehensive systemic evaluation includes syphilis serology (RPR/VDRL and FTA-ABS or TPPA — mandatory in every bilateral uveitis workup), tuberculin skin test or IGRA, chest X-ray or CT (sarcoidosis), serum ACE and lysozyme (sarcoidosis), ANA and anti-dsDNA (lupus), HLA-B51 (Behçet), and a uveitis specialist referral. VKH is diagnosed primarily on clinical grounds using the revised international diagnostic criteria, which incorporate bilateral granulomatous panuveitis, neurological, auditory, and skin findings. Behçet diagnosis similarly uses the International Study Group criteria. Intraocular fluid sampling by anterior chamber paracentesis or vitreous biopsy for PCR and cytology is reserved for atypical or unresponsive cases.

diagnosticTests.test: Slit-lamp biomicroscopy with dilated fundus exam
diagnosticTests.purpose: Directly measures anterior chamber and vitreous inflammation, identifies keratic precipitates, posterior synechiae, choroidal lesions, retinal vasculitis, and exudative detachments essential for diagnosis and activity grading.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Your pupils are dilated with eye drops (blurring vision for 4-6 hours). The ophthalmologist shines a bright narrow beam of light into each eye while you look in different directions. The examination takes 20-30 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects and precisely quantifies macular oedema, the primary cause of central vision loss in panuveitis, and monitors response to treatment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A non-contact scan of the back of your eye; you look into a machine and a light scans your retina. The procedure is painless and takes about five minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: Fluorescein angiography (FA) and indocyanine green angiography (ICGA)
diagnosticTests.purpose: FA reveals retinal vascular leakage, disc oedema, and areas of capillary non-perfusion; ICGA shows choroidal granulomata and hypofluorescent dark dots in VKH and sarcoidosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A fluorescent dye is injected into a vein in your arm; photographs are taken of the retina as the dye passes through. A mild taste or brief nausea is common. Rarely an allergic reaction occurs.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Mandatory first-line test in every bilateral uveitis to exclude syphilis, which is fully treatable and mimics virtually any form of uveitis including bilateral panuveitis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results available within 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Interferon-gamma release assay (IGRA) or tuberculin skin test
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for prior Mycobacterium tuberculosis exposure as a cause of or contributor to bilateral uveitis; a positive result in the appropriate clinical context supports a trial of anti-TB therapy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: IGRA is a blood test with results in one to two days. The tuberculin skin test involves a small injection under the forearm skin with the reaction read at 48-72 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum ACE, lysozyme, and chest imaging
diagnosticTests.purpose: Elevated ACE and lysozyme, combined with hilar lymphadenopathy on chest X-ray or CT, support a diagnosis of sarcoidosis as the cause of bilateral panuveitis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw and standard chest imaging; ACE and lysozyme results return within 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: HLA-B51 genotyping and Behçet disease clinical criteria assessment
diagnosticTests.purpose: HLA-B51 is found in approximately 60% of Behçet disease patients and supports the diagnosis when combined with the clinical picture of recurrent aphthous ulcers, genital ulcers, and ocular inflammation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood sample sent for genetic analysis; results take several days.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of bilateral panuveitis must address both the ocular inflammation and the underlying systemic cause. For non-infectious panuveitis, the backbone of treatment is corticosteroids — topical (prednisolone acetate eye drops), periocular (sub-Tenon's triamcinolone injection), intravitreal (triamcinolone or dexamethasone implants), and systemic (oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day for VKH; intravenous methylprednisolone in severe or vision-threatening attacks). Systemic corticosteroids are typically tapered slowly over weeks to months, guided by inflammatory markers and slit-lamp findings. For conditions requiring prolonged control or where corticosteroids cannot be tapered below an acceptable dose (typically below 7.5-10 mg/day prednisolone equivalent), steroid-sparing immunosuppressants are added: methotrexate, mycophenolate mofetil, and azathioprine are first-line immunomodulatory agents; cyclosporin, tacrolimus, and cyclophosphamide are used for refractory cases. Biologic agents — infliximab and adalimumab — have demonstrated efficacy in Behçet disease and refractory non-infectious panuveitis; adalimumab (Humira) is FDA- and EMA-approved for non-infectious intermediate, posterior, and panuveitis. Infectious causes are treated with pathogen-specific agents: intravenous benzathine penicillin for syphilis, anti-TB regimens for ocular TB, and ganciclovir or foscarnet for CMV. Macular oedema causing central vision loss is treated with intravitreal anti-VEGF agents or intravitreal dexamethasone implants. Complications such as cataract, glaucoma, and epiretinal membrane are addressed surgically after inflammation is controlled.

medicalTreatments.name: Systemic corticosteroids (oral prednisolone)
medicalTreatments.description: High-dose oral prednisolone, typically 1 mg/kg/day up to 80 mg/day, initiated for acute bilateral non-infectious panuveitis — particularly VKH disease — with a slow taper over weeks to months guided by clinical response. Often combined with a steroid-sparing agent for long-term control.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Oral corticosteroids achieve initial inflammation control in approximately 80-90% of VKH and sarcoid panuveitis cases; tapering below 10 mg/day without a steroid-sparing agent leads to relapse in the majority.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravitreal dexamethasone implant (Ozurdex)
medicalTreatments.description: A sustained-release 0.7 mg biodegradable intravitreal dexamethasone implant delivering anti-inflammatory drug to the posterior segment for up to six months, bypassing systemic side effects. Particularly effective for macular oedema in panuveitis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: In the HURON trial, 47% of patients receiving Ozurdex achieved a vitreous haze score of 0 at 8 weeks versus 12% on sham; median best-corrected visual acuity improved by approximately 10 ETDRS letters.

medicalTreatments.name: Methotrexate (steroid-sparing immunosuppression)
medicalTreatments.description: An antimetabolite that inhibits dihydrofolate reductase, suppressing T-cell and B-cell proliferation. Given weekly at 10-25 mg orally or subcutaneously with folic acid supplementation for non-infectious panuveitis requiring long-term corticosteroid reduction.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves corticosteroid reduction to below 10 mg/day in approximately 70-80% of non-infectious uveitis patients at 12 months in observational series.

medicalTreatments.name: Adalimumab (anti-TNF biologic)
medicalTreatments.description: A monoclonal antibody targeting tumour necrosis factor-alpha, approved for non-infectious intermediate, posterior, and panuveitis. Dosed at 80 mg subcutaneous loading, then 40 mg every two weeks. Particularly effective in Behçet disease and refractory non-infectious panuveitis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: In the VISUAL I trial, adalimumab reduced treatment failure by approximately 50% relative risk reduction compared with placebo in active non-infectious uveitis at 24 weeks.

medicalTreatments.name: Benzathine penicillin G (for syphilitic panuveitis)
medicalTreatments.description: Neurosyphilis/ocular syphilis is treated with intravenous aqueous crystalline penicillin G 18-24 million units per day for 10-14 days per CDC guidelines, as ocular involvement is considered CNS disease requiring CSF-penetrating doses.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete resolution of uveitis occurs in approximately 80-90% of patients with syphilitic uveitis treated with adequate penicillin; visual prognosis is excellent when treatment is early.

surgicalOptions.name: Pars plana vitrectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical removal of the vitreous gel to clear dense vitreous haze, obtain vitreous samples for diagnosis in culture-negative cases, manage vitreous haemorrhage, or treat traction retinal detachment complicating panuveitis.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Diagnostic vitrectomy improves aetiological identification by approximately 25-40% in culture-negative cases; vitreous clearing improves visual acuity in approximately 60-70% of appropriately selected patients.

surgicalOptions.name: Cataract surgery
surgicalOptions.description: Posterior subcapsular cataracts develop in a high proportion of panuveitis patients, both from inflammation and from long-term corticosteroid use. Phacoemulsification with intraocular lens implantation is performed once inflammation is controlled for at least three months.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Visual outcomes are good when performed with inactive inflammation; vision improves in approximately 75-85% of cases, though uveitis flares after surgery occur in 20-40% and require perioperative immunosuppression intensification.

surgicalOptions.name: Glaucoma filtering surgery (trabeculectomy or drainage implant)
surgicalOptions.description: Secondary glaucoma from angle synechiae, steroid-induced raised intraocular pressure, or inflammatory damage to the trabecular meshwork may require surgical drainage to preserve the optic nerve when medical therapy is insufficient.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Long-term IOP control achieved in approximately 60-75% of uveitic glaucoma cases; repeated procedures may be required, and outcomes are less favourable than in primary glaucoma.

alternativeTreatments.name: Sub-Tenon's corticosteroid injection
alternativeTreatments.description: Triamcinolone acetonide 40 mg injected into the sub-Tenon's space behind the eye delivers high local drug concentration to the posterior segment with lower systemic exposure than oral steroids; a useful adjunct for unilateral exacerbations or to spare systemic dose.

alternativeTreatments.name: Intravitreal anti-VEGF therapy
alternativeTreatments.description: Bevacizumab, ranibizumab, or aflibercept injections targeting vascular endothelial growth factor reduce macular oedema and neovascularisation complicating panuveitis when corticosteroids alone are insufficient or contraindicated.

whySeeSpecialist: Bilateral panuveitis always warrants immediate referral to a uveitis specialist ophthalmologist. The condition threatens vision in both eyes, the differential diagnosis spans infectious and autoimmune systemic diseases, treatment decisions between immunosuppression and antibiotics depend on accurate aetiology, and frequent slit-lamp monitoring with OCT is essential for guiding therapy. Co-management by a rheumatologist, infectious disease specialist, or immunologist is frequently required for systemic conditions driving the uveitis.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek an ophthalmologist with subspecialty training in uveitis, ideally affiliated with a tertiary referral centre with access to fluorescein and ICG angiography, OCT, and intravitreal delivery facilities. In the UK, a consultant ophthalmologist with an uveitis interest within an NHS tertiary service is appropriate. Rheumatological co-management by a physician experienced in sarcoidosis, Behçet disease, or autoimmune conditions enhances long-term outcomes. For infectious causes, an infectious disease physician ensures adequate systemic treatment of syphilis or TB.
preventionStrategies: Screen and treat syphilis promptly — serological testing and penicillin treatment prevent ocular involvement.
Complete TB treatment and follow national TB screening recommendations in high-prevalence settings to prevent ocular TB.
Manage systemic inflammatory conditions such as sarcoidosis and Behçet disease in partnership with specialists to reduce ocular flare frequency.
After penetrating ocular trauma, seek immediate ophthalmological assessment and maintain vigilance for symptoms in the uninjured eye, which may develop sympathetic ophthalmia weeks to years later.
Wear appropriate eye protection in workplaces and sports with penetrating injury risk to prevent sympathetic ophthalmia.
For patients on long-term systemic corticosteroids, regular intraocular pressure monitoring reduces the risk of undetected steroid-induced glaucoma.
lifestyleModifications: Attend all scheduled ophthalmology appointments; disease activity monitoring with the slit lamp is the only way to detect subclinical inflammation that can silently damage the retina.
Do not reduce or stop immunosuppressive medications without specialist guidance — uveitis frequently relapses asymptomatically.
Wear UV-protective sunglasses outdoors to reduce photophobic discomfort and limit ultraviolet stimulation of the inflamed uveal tissue.
Monitor and report new floaters, sudden vision change, or pain immediately — these may signal a flare or complication requiring urgent treatment.
Manage systemic comorbidities such as hypertension and diabetes carefully, as both worsen macular oedema associated with uveitis.

recommended: Anti-inflammatory diet patterns — Mediterranean diet with high olive oil, oily fish, vegetables, and whole grains — supported by general observational evidence for systemic inflammatory diseases
Adequate calcium and vitamin D supplementation for patients on long-term systemic corticosteroids to reduce bone mineral density loss
High-antioxidant foods (leafy greens, berries) to support retinal health
Adequate hydration to support ocular surface health and general wellbeing
avoid: Excess alcohol, which interacts with methotrexate and may increase liver toxicity
High-salt processed foods, which worsen systemic corticosteroid-induced hypertension and fluid retention
Smoking — associated with worse outcomes in several systemic inflammatory diseases driving panuveitis

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate exercise is beneficial for managing systemic inflammatory diseases that cause bilateral panuveitis. Avoid high-impact or contact sports during flares or immediately after intraocular injections or surgery. Swimming is best avoided within two weeks of intravitreal injections to reduce infection risk. Otherwise, regular physical activity is encouraged and supports metabolic health, which is important for patients on long-term corticosteroids.
dailyManagement: Use topical corticosteroid eye drops exactly as prescribed — frequency is critical; do not taper without ophthalmologist guidance.
Monitor intraocular pressure regularly if on topical or systemic corticosteroids to detect steroid-induced glaucoma early.
Report any sudden worsening of vision, new floaters, or eye pain to your ophthalmologist without delay.
Keep a symptom diary tracking floaters, visual acuity at home with each eye, and any new systemic symptoms relevant to the underlying disease.
Ensure immunosuppressive blood tests (CBC, liver function, renal function) are performed at intervals specified by the prescribing team.
prognosis: The visual prognosis for bilateral panuveitis depends critically on the underlying cause, speed of diagnosis, and adequacy of treatment. VKH disease treated with early high-dose corticosteroids achieves visual acuity of 20/40 or better in 65-80% of patients; delayed treatment carries a much worse prognosis with disc damage and atrophic patches. Behçet disease panuveitis is among the most visually destructive: without adequate immunosuppression with biologics, up to 25% of patients become legally blind within five years due to cumulative ischaemic damage. Syphilitic panuveitis treated promptly with intravenous penicillin resolves completely in approximately 80-90% of cases. Sarcoid and TB-related panuveitis generally respond well to treatment, with visual preservation in the majority. The key determinants of good outcome are identification of the aetiology, early initiation of appropriate therapy, and maintenance of suppression to prevent relapses.
recoveryTimeline: Acute VKH disease treated promptly shows anterior segment quieting within two to four weeks of high-dose corticosteroids; posterior segment changes (subretinal fluid, disc oedema) typically resolve over four to eight weeks. Long-term immunosuppression is required for months to years to prevent recurrence. Behçet disease requires indefinite treatment. After a treated infectious cause (syphilis, TB), inflammation resolves over four to twelve weeks. Macular oedema responds to intravitreal therapy over four to eight weeks with serial OCT monitoring. Some residual visual loss from macular scars or optic atrophy may persist after even well-treated bilateral panuveitis.
complications: Cystoid macular oedema, the most common cause of central visual loss in bilateral panuveitis, arising from breakdown of the blood-retinal barrier.
Posterior subcapsular cataract from chronic inflammation and corticosteroid exposure, occurring in the majority of patients with longstanding panuveitis.
Secondary glaucoma from peripheral anterior synechiae, pupillary block, or steroid-induced elevation of intraocular pressure, threatening optic nerve damage.
Exudative retinal detachment in severe acute VKH or sympathetic ophthalmia, caused by large choroidal effusions lifting the retina without a tear.
Optic nerve atrophy from prolonged disc oedema or ischaemic vasculitis in Behçet disease, causing permanent loss of peripheral and central visual field.
Phthisis bulbi — end-stage shrinkage and dysfunction of the eye — following repeated severe uveitic attacks without adequate treatment.

supportResources.name: American Uveitis Society
supportResources.description: Professional society providing patient education resources on uveitis diagnosis, treatment, and research.

supportResources.name: RNIB — Eye conditions: Uveitis
supportResources.description: UK Royal National Institute of Blind People patient information page on uveitis symptoms, causes, and living with the condition.

supportResources.name: NEI — Facts About Uveitis
supportResources.description: Overview of uveitis from the U.S. National Eye Institute covering types, symptoms, and treatment.

sources.title: Jabs DA et al. Standardization of Uveitis Nomenclature (SUN) Working Group. Nomenclature for Uveitis. Am J Ophthalmol, 2005
Nguyen QD et al. Adalimumab for Prevention of Uveitic Flare in Patients with Inactive Non-infectious Uveitis (VISUAL II). Lancet, 2016

confusedWithConditions.name: Sympathetic uveitis, bilateral
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Sympathetic ophthalmia is a specific bilateral granulomatous panuveitis triggered by prior penetrating ocular trauma or surgery; it shares the bilateral panuveitis pattern but has a distinct post-traumatic aetiology.

confusedWithConditions.name: Endophthalmitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Endophthalmitis is infection of the entire vitreous cavity and anterior segment, typically post-surgical or post-traumatic, presenting with severe acute pain and hypopyon; it is infectious rather than immune-mediated and requires intravitreal antibiotics or antifungals.

confusedWithConditions.name: Sarcoidosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Sarcoidosis is a common systemic cause of panuveitis; the ocular manifestation is diagnosed as panuveitis, while sarcoidosis is the underlying systemic disease — both diagnoses may apply simultaneously.

relatedConditions.name: Sympathetic uveitis, bilateral
relatedConditions.relevance: A specific form of bilateral granulomatous panuveitis with a post-traumatic immunological trigger; shares treatment principles and slit-lamp features with other panuveitis causes.

relatedConditions.name: Panuveitis, right eye
relatedConditions.relevance: Unilateral panuveitis that may precede bilateral involvement or remain unilateral depending on aetiology.

relatedConditions.name: Panuveitis, left eye
relatedConditions.relevance: Unilateral left-eye panuveitis; managed with the same anatomical and aetiological framework as bilateral disease.

relatedConditions.name: Other endophthalmitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Infectious pan-ocular inflammation with overlapping clinical features requiring prompt distinction from immune-mediated panuveitis to direct appropriate treatment.

relatedConditions.name: Sarcoidosis
relatedConditions.relevance: One of the most common systemic causes of bilateral panuveitis; chest imaging and ACE level testing are part of the standard uveitis workup for this reason.

faqs.question: What causes bilateral panuveitis?
faqs.answer: Bilateral panuveitis is nearly always caused by a systemic condition. The most common causes are sarcoidosis, Vogt-Koyanagi-Harada disease, Behçet disease, tuberculosis, and syphilis. In rare cases sympathetic ophthalmia follows an eye injury. Finding the underlying cause is essential because treatment differs markedly between infectious and inflammatory types.

faqs.question: Is bilateral panuveitis serious?
faqs.answer: Yes. Bilateral panuveitis threatens vision in both eyes. Approximately 35% of patients experience significant vision loss over five years without adequate treatment. It requires urgent specialist care. With prompt diagnosis and appropriate treatment — whether corticosteroids, immunosuppressants, or antibiotics — vision can be preserved in most cases.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of panuveitis?
faqs.answer: Blurred vision, eye pain, photophobia, and floaters are the main symptoms. Redness and tearing also occur. In bilateral panuveitis both eyes are affected, though severity may differ. Headache, ringing in the ears, or skin colour changes alongside the eye symptoms suggest Vogt-Koyanagi-Harada disease.

faqs.question: How is panuveitis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: An ophthalmologist examines both eyes with a slit lamp and dilated fundus examination, measures intraocular pressure, and performs OCT scanning to detect macular swelling. Blood tests for syphilis, TB, sarcoidosis, and autoimmune markers are standard. Fluorescein angiography shows retinal vascular leakage.

faqs.question: What is Vogt-Koyanagi-Harada disease?
faqs.answer: VKH disease is an autoimmune condition in which the immune system attacks pigment-containing cells in the eyes, inner ear, meninges, and skin. It produces bilateral panuveitis with exudative retinal detachments, often alongside headache, hearing changes, and skin depigmentation. It responds well to early high-dose corticosteroids.

faqs.question: Can panuveitis cause permanent blindness?
faqs.answer: Untreated or poorly controlled bilateral panuveitis can cause permanent vision loss through macular scarring, optic nerve damage, cataracts, and glaucoma. Behçet disease carries the highest risk of blindness among common causes. Regular specialist follow-up and adherence to treatment greatly reduce this risk.

faqs.question: How is panuveitis treated?
faqs.answer: Non-infectious panuveitis is treated with corticosteroids — eye drops, injections around the eye, or oral tablets — and steroid-sparing immunosuppressants such as methotrexate for long-term control. Adalimumab is approved for refractory non-infectious uveitis. Infectious causes such as syphilis or TB need pathogen-specific antibiotics.

faqs.question: Can syphilis cause panuveitis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Syphilis is one of the most important causes of bilateral uveitis including panuveitis and must be excluded by blood test in every new uveitis workup. Syphilitic panuveitis is fully reversible with intravenous penicillin. Missing this diagnosis leads to progressive, preventable vision loss.

faqs.question: How often will I need ophthalmology appointments for panuveitis?
faqs.answer: During active disease or treatment changes, visits are typically every two to four weeks. When well controlled, appointments are every one to three months. Regular monitoring is essential because inflammation can be subclinically active, causing damage without obvious symptoms, and medication side effects such as raised eye pressure need surveillance.

faqs.question: Is panuveitis the same as uveitis?
faqs.answer: Uveitis is the broad term for inflammation of any part of the uveal tract. Panuveitis is a specific severe subtype involving all three uveal layers simultaneously — the iris, ciliary body, and choroid — plus the vitreous. It is the most extensive anatomical form and generally the most vision-threatening type of uveitis.

faqs.question: What eye drops are used for panuveitis?
faqs.answer: Topical prednisolone acetate 1% eye drops, applied frequently (every 1-2 hours initially) for anterior inflammation, are the mainstay of front-of-eye treatment. Cycloplegic drops such as atropine or cyclopentolate relieve ciliary spasm pain and prevent posterior synechiae. Additional intravitreal or systemic medication manages posterior segment involvement.

faqs.question: Can I drive if I have bilateral panuveitis?
faqs.answer: This depends on your current visual acuity and visual field in both eyes. You must meet the legal visual standard for driving in your country. Active bilateral panuveitis with significant visual blurring, macular oedema, or photophobia may temporarily impair driving safety. Discuss driving suitability at each ophthalmology appointment.

faqs.question: What is a sub-Tenon's injection?
faqs.answer: A sub-Tenon's injection delivers corticosteroid (triamcinolone) directly behind the eye into the sub-Tenon's space, providing high local drug concentration to the posterior segment. After local anaesthetic drops, a fine cannula is passed through a small opening in the conjunctiva. The effect lasts several weeks to months.

faqs.question: Does panuveitis run in families?
faqs.answer: Most forms of panuveitis are not directly inherited, but genetic factors influence susceptibility. VKH disease is associated with HLA-DR4 alleles. Behçet disease is linked to HLA-B51. Sarcoidosis has familial clustering. Having a first-degree relative with any of these conditions modestly raises your own risk.

faqs.question: What blood tests are done when panuveitis is first diagnosed?
faqs.answer: The standard initial panel includes syphilis serology (RPR and FTA-ABS or TPPA), IGRA or tuberculin test for TB exposure, serum ACE and chest imaging for sarcoidosis, full blood count, CRP, ANA, anti-dsDNA, and HLA-B51 if Behçet disease is possible. The uveitis specialist tailors the workup to the clinical pattern.

faqs.question: How long does panuveitis treatment last?
faqs.answer: This depends on the underlying cause. Syphilitic panuveitis resolves in weeks with penicillin. VKH disease typically requires 12-24 months of immunosuppression to prevent recurrence, and some patients need indefinite treatment. Behçet disease requires long-term biologic therapy. Regular specialist review guides duration.

faqs.question: What happens if panuveitis is not treated?
faqs.answer: Without treatment, bilateral panuveitis progressively damages the retina through macular oedema and scarring, raises eye pressure causing glaucoma, clouds the lens causing cataract, and in severe cases leads to permanent bilateral blindness. In Behçet disease, retinal vessel occlusion can destroy central vision within months of onset.

faqs.question: What is the difference between panuveitis and endophthalmitis?
faqs.answer: Both involve inflammation of all intraocular structures, but endophthalmitis is caused by bacterial or fungal infection — usually post-surgical or post-traumatic — and presents acutely with severe pain, hypopyon, and rapid vision loss within hours to days. Panuveitis is typically immune-mediated and develops more gradually. Treatment for endophthalmitis is urgent intravitreal antibiotics.

faqs.question: Can diet or supplements help panuveitis?
faqs.answer: No diet or supplement is proven to treat panuveitis. However, patients on long-term corticosteroids need adequate calcium (1,000-1,200 mg/day) and vitamin D to prevent osteoporosis. A Mediterranean-style diet may support general inflammatory control. Smoking cessation is strongly recommended as smoking worsens systemic inflammatory diseases.

faqs.question: When should I seek emergency care for panuveitis?
faqs.answer: Seek emergency eye care if you notice sudden vision loss, see a curtain across your vision (possible retinal detachment), experience severe eye pain with a hard-feeling eye (acute glaucoma), or see a white fluid level inside the eye (hypopyon). These signs require same-day or emergency ophthalmological assessment.

metaTitle: Bilateral Panuveitis: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Bilateral panuveitis inflames all uveal layers in both eyes, threatening vision. Learn causes like VKH, Behçet, sarcoidosis, diagnosis, and treatment options.
